title: Wood-wick soy candle
subtitle: A crackling soy container candle with a flat wooden wick and a warm fragrance
excerpt: A flat wooden wick produces a gentle, low crackling sound as it burns. The effect is subtler than a wood fire but distinctive. Wood wicks need a wider candle jar than cotton wicks of equivalent size; this formula uses a wide-mouth jar to ensure a proper melt pool.
sourceNotes: Wood-wick sizing guide from CandleScience (candlescience.com, May 2026). Wood wick behaviour and trim requirements from Wooden Wick Co. guide (woodenwick.com). Soy wax performance with wood wicks from Gracefruit fragrance-making guide (gracefruit.com, May 2026).

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 container candle (~200 g wax in a wide 250 ml jar)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Test one candle before committing to a full batch; wood wick sizing varies by jar diameter and wax blend.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure wood-wick soy candles for at least 1 day before the first burn; 3 days gives the best performance. Wood wicks require a longer cure than cotton wicks for reliable crackling.
recipe.temperatureNote: Add fragrance when the wax has cooled to 65 degrees Celsius. Pour at 60 degrees Celsius; soy wax poured hotter than 65 degrees Celsius tends to sink in the centre as it cools and leaves the wood wick exposed on one side.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years in a cool, dark place with the lid on. Trim the wood wick to around 4 mm before each burn; a longer wick produces a larger flame and excessive flickering rather than crackling.

recipeTools.notes: Essential for wood wicks, which sit flat and need steady centring

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of fragrance or essential oil by weight relative to the wax. This formula uses 8% fragrance load, the standard for soy container wax.

When a candle burns down the centre without melting the surrounding wax to the jar edges. Common when the wick is too small for the jar diameter or when the first burn was cut too short. Wood wicks are more prone to tunnelling than cotton wicks if the jar diameter is not matched to the wick width.
The first burn of a candle, which sets the maximum melt pool diameter for all subsequent burns. A full first burn (melt pool reaches the jar edge) prevents tunnelling.

content.text: Wood wicks are flat, porous strips that draw wax by capillary action across their width rather than up a round cotton core. The crackling sound comes from the wood fibres expanding and contracting as moisture in the wick material releases during combustion. The effect is mild rather than dramatic; think of it as a lighter, cleaner version of a fire rather than a roaring hearth.

Wood wick sizing is the key variable. A wick that is too narrow produces a small flame that drowns in wax; a wick that is too wide produces excessive flickering and soot. The standard approach is to test one candle before a full batch. This formula uses a medium wood wick (approximately 11 mm wide, 3 mm thick) suitable for a 7 to 8 cm diameter jar. If the wick extinguishes itself within 30 minutes of lighting, step up to the next width. The
is cedarwood and sweet orange, chosen for their stability in wax and warm character.

items.prepNote: 5% of wax weight; the base note

content.text: Pre-tab the wood wick with a metal sustainer tab before starting. Soak the lower 5 mm of the wick in a small amount of melted wax and allow to dry; this primes the wood and improves initial lighting. Fix the wick tab to the centre of the jar base. Use a centring bar to hold the flat wick upright and centred.

Melt the soy wax in the pouring pitcher over a simmering-water bath. Allow to cool to 65 degrees Celsius. Stir in both essential oils. Pour slowly at 60 to 65 degrees Celsius, keeping the wick centred. Leave undisturbed for a minimum of 1 day. The candle should cure for 3 days before the first burn.

for a wood-wick candle is even more important than for a cotton-wick candle. Allow the melt pool to reach the jar edge on the first burn. For a 7 to 8 cm jar this takes 3 to 4 hours. Do not trim the wick before the first burn; wait until it has burned once and cooled, then break away any charred mushroom cap and trim to 4 mm. If
occurs despite a full first burn, the wick is too small for the jar; step up one width on the next batch.
2 years in a cool, dark place with the lid on. Trim the wick to 4 mm before each burn. A wood wick that has been used once is ready for the next burn without soaking.
